name: Badakhshan Mountains & Lakes
desc_short: Alpine peaks and sapphire glacial lakes.
desc_long: A realm of extreme geography where the Hindu Kush meets the Pamirs. Badakhshan's landscapes feature dramatic vertical walls, deep valleys, and high-altitude basins where seasonal lakes shift through shades of turquoise and deep sapphire. It is a premier destination for expedition-style travel and remote mountain photography.

name: The Wakhan Corridor
desc_short: The legendary 'Roof of the World'.
desc_long: Stretching between the Pamirs and the Hindu Kush, the Wakhan Corridor is a high-altitude panhandle that once served as a vital Silk Road link. This isolated region offers a rare sense of scale, characterized by broad river valleys, ancient ruins, and the semi-nomadic cultures of the Wakhi and Kyrgyz people.

name: Mount Noshaq
desc_short: Afghanistan's highest and mightiest peak.
desc_long: Standing at 7,492 meters, Mount Noshaq is the crown of the Hindu Kush. While its summit is reserved for serious mountaineers, the surrounding base camps and glacier-fed valleys provide trekkers with some of the most awe-inspiring high-alpine scenery in Central Asia.

name: Badghis Hills & Nomadic Pastures
desc_short: Rolling pistachio hills and nomadic life.
desc_long: Badghis is famous for its rolling green hills and wild pistachio forests. The landscape serves as a vital grazing ground for nomadic Kuchi tribes, offering a serene and pastoral view of Afghanistan that transforms from vibrant green in spring to a golden velvet in late summer.

name: Surkh Kotal
desc_short: Majestic ruins of the Kushan Empire.
desc_long: Commonly known as the 'Red Pass,' this 1st-century CE archaeological site features a monumental temple complex built by King Kanishka. Its tiered hill-top structure offers a powerful visual connection to the region's pre-Islamic imperial history and the blending of Greek and Bactrian architectural styles.

name: Shrine of Hazrat Ali (Blue Mosque)
desc_short: A turquoise masterpiece of Islamic art.
desc_long: The spiritual heart of Mazar-e-Sharif, this world-renowned shrine is draped in thousands of intricate turquoise and azure tiles. Beyond its stunning symmetry and architecture, it is a vibrant cultural hub where white pigeons flock and pilgrims gather in a peaceful atmosphere of devotion.

name: Ancient City of Balkh
desc_short: The historic 'Mother of Cities'.
desc_long: As one of the oldest cities in the world, Balkh (ancient Bactria) was a spiritual center for Zoroastrianism and Buddhism long before the Islamic era. Visitors can explore massive sun-dried mud-brick walls, the ruins of the Green Mosque, and layers of history that define the Silk Road.

name: Tomb of Rabia Balkhi
desc_short: A shrine to Persian literature's first poet.
desc_long: This modest, elegant tomb commemorates Rabia Balkhi, a 10th-century poet who is a legendary figure in Persian literature. Located within the ancient ruins of Balkh, the site is a place of pilgrimage for those wishing to honor her tragic life and enduring poetic legacy.

name: Haji Piyada (Nine Domes) Mosque
desc_short: Afghanistan's oldest surviving mosque.
desc_long: Dating back to the 9th century, this humble yet historically vital structure is a rare example of early Samanid-style architecture. Its remaining columns and exquisite carved stucco work offer a quiet, profound glimpse into the early Islamic artistic traditions of Central Asia.

name: Rumi's Father's House (Madrasa)
desc_short: The ancestral home of the great mystic Rumi.
desc_long: This mud-brick ruin is traditionally believed to be the home and madrasa of Bahauddin Walad, the father of the world-famous Sufi poet Jalaluddin Rumi. It serves as a symbolic bridge to the golden age of Balkh as a global center of spirituality and learning.

name: Qala-i-Jangi Fortress
desc_short: Massive 19th-century earthwork fortress.
desc_long: Located near Mazar-e-Sharif, this sprawling fortress complex is an imposing example of modern-era Afghan military architecture. Built from sun-baked brick and earth, its high walls and massive gates have witnessed centuries of regional power struggles and strategic military history.

name: Buddha Niches of Bamyan
desc_short: Iconic Silk Road cultural giants.
desc_long: Carved into the sandstone cliffs in the 6th century, these massive niches once housed the world’s tallest standing Buddhas. Today, they remain a UNESCO World Heritage site and a powerful symbol of Afghanistan's diverse history, offering panoramic views of the valley from the honeycomb of caves and monastic cells.

name: Shahr-e-Gholghola
desc_short: The haunting 'City of Screams' ruins.
desc_long: Overlooking the Bamyan Valley, this ruined citadel bears silent witness to the Mongol invasion of 1221. Walking through its crumbling towers and residential alleys offers a somber historical narrative and the best 360-degree views of the Buddha niches and the surrounding peaks.

name: Shahr-e-Zohak
desc_short: The dramatic 'Red City' mountain fortress.
desc_long: Perched at the junction of the Bamyan and Kalu rivers, this 'Red City' is named for the stunning rust-colored volcanic rock on which it sits. Its series of towers and defensive walls seem to grow out of the cliffs, providing a formidable example of medieval Silk Road defense.

name: Band-e-Amir National Park
desc_short: Stunning sapphire-blue travertine lakes.
desc_long: Often called 'The Grand Canyon of Afghanistan,' this national park features six natural dams made of travertine. The contrast between the deep, electric-blue water and the towering, sun-scorched limestone cliffs creates one of the most breathtaking natural spectacles in the world.

name: Koh-e-Baba Mountain Range
desc_short: The snowy spine of the Central Highlands.
desc_long: Part of the Hindu Kush system, the Koh-e-Baba range is the watershed of Afghanistan. It offers rugged alpine beauty for hikers in the summer and a growing destination for winter sports, with peaks providing a stark, beautiful backdrop to the Bamyan Valley.

name: Dragon Valley
desc_short: Nature's petrified 'Dragon' ridge.
desc_long: Named after a distinctive rock formation that resembles a petrified dragon, this valley is steeped in local folklore. A walk along the 'spine' of the dragon reveals mineral springs and unique geological textures, making it a quick and rewarding nature excursion near Bamyan.

name: Chehel Burj Bamyan Ruins
desc_short: The mysterious 'Forty Towers' fortress.
desc_long: Chehel Burj is a formidable ruined fortress located in a remote mountain setting. Its name, meaning 'Forty Towers,' refers to the numerous defensive structures that ring the site. It offers an atmospheric experience for travelers seeking history off the beaten path.

name: Daykundi Highlands
desc_short: Terraced valleys and almond blossoms.
desc_long: Daykundi is a stunning region of the Hazarajat highlands, known for its terraced mountainside farming and high-quality almonds. The province offers a peaceful glimpse into rural Afghan life, where stone houses cling to hillsides above rushing mountain streams.

name: Farah Citadel
desc_short: An ancient river-side desert fortress.
desc_long: Steeped in legend and associated with the era of Alexander the Great, this massive mud-brick citadel stands guard over the Farah River. Its towering ramparts provide a stark silhouette against the desert horizon and offer a historic perspective on western trade routes.

name: Faryab Cultural Villages
desc_short: Turkmen heritage and artisan weaving.
desc_long: Faryab's villages are the heart of Afghanistan's northern carpet-weaving traditions. Visitors can experience the unique Turkmen culture, traditional yurt-style homes, and vibrant local markets where world-class hand-knotted rugs have been traded for generations.

name: Ghazni Minarets
desc_short: Ornate 12th-century victory towers.
desc_long: Two stunning brick minarets stand as the last major remnants of the Ghaznavid Empire's glory. Decorated with complex geometric patterns and Kufic inscriptions, they represent a pinnacle of medieval Islamic architecture and the former might of Sultan Mahmud's capital.

name: Bala Hisar of Ghazni
desc_short: The historic high citadel of the south.
desc_long: A formidable fortress that has overlooked Ghazni for over a millennium. Its walls have sheltered empires from the Ghaznavids to the Mughals, and it remains a key site for understanding the strategic military geography of the central-southern highlands.

name: Tomb of Sultan Mahmud
desc_short: Resting place of a medieval world-conqueror.
desc_long: Located in a quiet garden setting, this tomb honors the most famous ruler of the Ghaznavid Empire. The site features intricate carved marble and serves as a significant historical anchor for the city's identity as a former imperial capital.

name: Tapa Sardar
desc_short: Remains of a massive hilltop stupa.
desc_long: One of the most important pre-Islamic sites in Afghanistan, Tapa Sardar was once a grand Buddhist monastery complex. Its ruins highlight the architectural sophistication of the 3rd to 8th centuries and Ghazni's role as a major stop on the Silk Road.

name: Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III
desc_short: Ruined grandeur of the Ghaznavid court.
desc_long: These sprawling ruins once formed a grand royal residence noted for its innovative use of brickwork and decorative terracotta. Today, the palace site provides archaeologists and travelers with a sense of the immense scale of medieval court life in the region.

name: Minaret of Jam
desc_short: A 12th-century masterpiece in the wilderness.
desc_long: Rising 65 meters above a remote river valley, this UNESCO World Heritage site is a miracle of medieval brickwork. Its intricate Kufic calligraphy and geometric patterns are perfectly preserved, standing as a lonely, towering testament to the height of the Ghurid Empire.

name: Rural Ghor Highlands
desc_short: Wild mountains and isolated stone villages.
desc_long: Ghor is one of Afghanistan's most rugged and least-traveled provinces. The landscape is defined by vast mountain plateaus and remote stone-built villages where life follows ancient seasonal rhythms, offering a true frontier experience for adventurous travelers.

name: Helmand River Landscape
desc_short: A lush desert lifeline and river oasis.
desc_long: The Helmand River creates a striking green ribbon through the arid southern plains. Along its banks, fertile fields of wheat and fruit orchards provide a vibrant contrast to the surrounding desert, showing how water has sustained life here for millennia.

name: Qala-e-Bost Arch
desc_short: Iconic Ghaznavid arch and desert citadel.
desc_long: One of the most famous architectural symbols of the south, this massive 11th-century arch once served as the grand entrance to a royal Ghaznavid city. Nearby, the ruins of a sprawling fortress offer a glimpse into the region's medieval importance.

name: Herat Old City & Bazaars
desc_short: A vibrant maze of silk and copper.
desc_long: Herat's Old City is a living museum. Within its narrow alleys, you'll find ancient silk-weaving workshops, copper-smiths hammering by hand, and spice markets that have remained active for centuries, maintaining the authentic rhythm of a Silk Road trading hub.

name: Herat Citadel
desc_short: Timeless fortress of Alexander the Great.
desc_long: Dating back to 330 BC, this massive citadel has been destroyed and rebuilt by countless conquerors, including Genghis Khan and Timur. Its imposing walls and towers dominate the Herat skyline, offering museum galleries and spectacular sunset views over the old city bazaars.

name: Herat Friday Mosque
desc_short: A dazzling jewel of Timurid tilework.
desc_long: One of the world's most beautiful mosques, this site is renowned for its vast courtyard and stunning blue tile mosaics. The mosque remains a living workspace, with an on-site tile workshop where artisans still use 15th-century techniques to maintain the structure.

name: Herat Minarets
desc_short: Striking silhouettes of a lost empire.
desc_long: The remaining minarets of the Musalla complex are a haunting reminder of the city's past as a center of the Timurid Renaissance. These towering brick structures, once part of a massive madrasa and mosque complex, offer one of Afghanistan's most iconic sunset views.

name: Tala Tepe
desc_short: Site of the famous 'Bactrian Gold'.
desc_long: Known as the 'Hill of Gold,' this archaeological site near Sheberghan was the discovery point of over 20,000 pieces of ancient gold jewelry. It highlights northern Afghanistan's role as a rich crossroads where Greek, Scythian, and Chinese cultures met 2,000 years ago.

name: Darulaman Palace
desc_short: Iconic symbol of national resilience.
desc_long: Built in the 1920s as part of King Amanullah Khan's modernization drive, this neoclassical palace has been meticulously restored after decades of war. It stands as a grand symbol of Kabul's architectural ambition and national renewal.

name: Kabul National Museum
desc_short: Treasury of Afghanistan's ancient past.
desc_long: Once one of the world's greatest museums, this institution has survived extraordinary odds to preserve the country's heritage. Its collections include Buddhist sculptures, ivory carvings, and Islamic art that provide essential context for the Silk Road's history.

name: Babur's Gardens
desc_short: Terraced Mughal paradise in Kabul.
desc_long: The final resting place of the first Mughal Emperor, Babur, these beautifully restored terraced gardens are an oasis of calm. Featuring marble pavilions, water channels, and ancient walnut trees, the site offers a glimpse into the refined lifestyle and garden-culture of the 16th-century Mughal court.

name: Shah-e-Du Shamshira Mosque
desc_short: The unique 'Mosque of Two Swords'.
desc_long: Located on the banks of the Kabul River, this 1920s mosque is architecturally unique for its Italian-influenced Baroque style. It stands on a historic site where legend says an early Muslim commander fought with two swords, making it a beloved city landmark.

name: Sakhi Shrine
desc_short: A vivid blue beacon of Kabul's faith.
desc_long: Nestled at the foot of Mount Asmai, this major shrine is famous for its vibrant blue and green tiles. It is a central place of worship for the city's Shi'a community and a peaceful space that offers a profound look at living religious traditions in the capital.

name: Wazir Akbar Khan Hill
desc_short: The best panoramic viewpoint in Kabul.
desc_long: This elevated hill offers the most famous view of the capital, marked by a massive Afghan flag. It is the perfect place to understand Kabul's sprawl and its dramatic mountain-ringed geography, especially at sunset when the city lights begin to twinkle.

name: Chicken Street
desc_short: Kabul's legendary antiques market.
desc_long: A historic corridor for travelers, Chicken Street is the go-to place for lapis lazuli jewelry, hand-woven carpets, and traditional Afghan crafts. Even for non-shoppers, it offers a fascinating look at the country's diverse artisanal heritage.

name: Qargha Lake
desc_short: Kabul's favorite weekend lakeside escape.
desc_long: Just a short drive from the city center, this reservoir is the primary recreation spot for Kabulis. With boat rides, lakeside restaurants, and fresh mountain air, it provides a relaxed, social atmosphere and a different side of life in the capital.

name: TV Tower Hill (Mount Asmai)
desc_short: A high-altitude gaze over the city.
desc_long: Known colloquially as TV Mountain, this peak bisects Kabul. A drive to its summit provides a stunning bird's-eye view of the city's neighborhoods and the ancient fortifications that wind along the surrounding mountain ridges.

name: Bala Hissar
desc_short: Kabul's ancient hilltop 'High Fortress'.
desc_long: Occupied for over 1,500 years, this fortress has been the seat of power for Kabul's rulers. Its thick stone walls and strategic location at the city's narrows offer a visible link to the centuries of conflict and trade that have defined the capital.

name: Kabul Zoo
desc_short: A resilient center of family life.
desc_long: While modest by international standards, the Kabul Zoo is a beloved public space. It houses species native to the region, such as the mountain snow leopard, and offers a glimpse into the everyday leisure lives of Kabul's residents.

name: Taq-e-Zafar Paghman
desc_short: European-style arch in a mountain retreat.
desc_long: Paghman is a high-altitude summer retreat for Kabulis, famous for its gardens and the 'Arch of Victory'. Modeled after the Arc de Triomphe, the monument commemorates Afghan independence and sits within a valley known for its fresh air and walnut groves.

name: Murad Khane
desc_short: Beautifully restored timber-framed old city.
desc_long: A hidden gem in Kabul's center, this neighborhood features exquisitely restored 18th-century cedar-wood buildings. It is a living example of traditional urban architecture and home to an institute that teaches ancient Afghan woodcarving and ceramic arts.

name: Tape Nader Khan
desc_short: Royal mausoleums on a quiet hill.
desc_long: This hilltop site contains the tombs of Afghanistan's 20th-century royal family, including King Nadir Shah. It offers a somber, regal atmosphere and provides excellent views of Kabul's eastern sprawl and the Olympic Stadium.

name: Kahfroshi Bird Market
desc_short: A sensory journey through the bird bazaar.
desc_long: This narrow, sun-dappled alleyway in old Kabul is one of the city's most atmospheric spots. Here, hundreds of songbirds in wicker cages line the walls, a tradition that reflects the Afghan love for bird-keeping and the timeless charm of the bazaar.

name: Ghazi Olympic Stadium
desc_short: The heart of Afghan sports and football.
desc_long: Named after King Amanullah Khan (the 'Ghazi'), this stadium is the primary venue for football and national events. It serves as a major reference point for public life in the capital and a symbol of the country's passion for sport.

name: OMAR Landmine Museum
desc_short: Educational insight into humanitarian work.
desc_long: This unique museum provides a vital look at the technical side of demining. Through its collection of hardware and instructional displays, it honors the deminers who have made Afghanistan safer and offers a perspective on the challenges of post-conflict recovery.

name: Khurd-Kabul Pass
desc_short: Dramatic mountain pass of military legend.
desc_long: This narrow gorge southeast of Kabul was the site of significant 19th-century battles. Its towering cliffs and winding stream offer a rugged natural beauty and a physical link to the 'Great Game' history that shaped the region.

name: Tomb of Ahmad Shah Durrani
desc_short: Shrine of the 'Father of Afghanistan'.
desc_long: This blue-domed mausoleum is the final resting place of Ahmad Shah Durrani, the founder of the modern Afghan state. Its interior is adorned with exquisite tilework and serves as a major landmark of national pride in the heart of Kandahar city.

name: Shrine of Mirwais Hotak
desc_short: Mausoleum of the Hotaki dynasty's founder.
desc_long: Set in a quiet garden in the Kokaran area, this tomb commemorates Mirwais Nika, the national hero who liberated the region in 1709. Its elegant architecture and peaceful setting make it a significant historical site for understanding Afghan political identity.

name: Baba Wali Shrine
desc_short: Hillside shrine with Arghandab Valley views.
desc_long: This multi-tiered shrine is perched on a hill overlooking the lush Arghandab Valley. It is one of Kandahar's most popular leisure spots, offering terraced gardens and spectacular views of the pomegranate orchards and river below.

name: Mosque of the Sacred Cloak
desc_short: The most revered spiritual site in Kandahar.
desc_long: This mosque houses the 'Kharqa Sharif', a cloak believed to have been worn by the Prophet Muhammad. It is the spiritual epicenter of the south, known for its intricate tilework and the deep reverence it commands from pilgrims across the nation.

name: Chilzina and the Forty Steps
desc_short: Ancient rock-cut stairs and royal views.
desc_long: Carved into a jagged mountain ridge by the Mughal Emperor Babur, these forty stone steps lead to a rock-cut chamber overlooking Kandahar. The site is historically significant for its Ashokan edicts and provides a commanding perspective of the city's unique geography.

name: Ayno Mina
desc_short: A model of modern Afghan urbanism.
desc_long: Ayno Mina is a premier planned community in Kandahar, featuring wide paved avenues, lush public parks, and contemporary architecture. It serves as a symbol of the city's reconstruction and provides a glimpse into modern residential life in the south.

name: Nijrab Valley
desc_short: Lush terraced hills and river springs.
desc_long: Nijrab is a stunning valley known for its fresh water springs and terraced mountainsides. It is an agricultural jewel near Kabul, where stone-walled villages are surrounded by almond and walnut trees, offering a refreshing and verdant landscape.

name: Khost City Scenery
desc_short: Pashtun heartland and rolling hills.
desc_long: Khost is defined by its vibrant Pashtun tribal culture and a landscape of rolling green hills that bridge the gap between the high mountains and the Indus plains. The region is known for its energetic markets and traditional hospitality.

name: Kunar River Valley
desc_short: Dramatic forests and rushing whitewater.
desc_long: Kunar is home to some of Afghanistan’s most ruggedly beautiful terrain. The valley is characterized by steep, pine-forested mountains and the powerful Kunar River, making it a spectacular destination for those who appreciate wild, vertical landscapes.

name: Kunduz City & Farmlands
desc_short: The fertile breadbasket of the north.
desc_long: Kunduz is a hub of northern agriculture, surrounded by vast, irrigated plains that produce much of the country's grain. The city's bazaars reflect this bounty and its position as a key link between central Afghanistan and the northern borders.

name: Alingar Valley
desc_short: A lush, river-fed agricultural oasis.
desc_long: The Alingar Valley is a masterpiece of rural scenery, where the river supports year-round farming. Traditional mud-brick homes are clustered amidst rice paddies and fruit orchards, providing a peaceful and highly photogenic eastern landscape.

name: Logar Valleys
desc_short: Fertile wheat fields and apple orchards.
desc_long: Just south of Kabul, Logar is a province of rich agricultural valleys. It is famous for its large orchards and historic villages, serving as a primary source of fruit for the capital and offering a serene rural landscape for travelers.

name: Jalalabad City
desc_short: The garden city of the east.
desc_long: Jalalabad is famous for its subtropical climate, orange groves, and public parks. As a major trading hub between Kabul and Peshawar, it possesses a unique energy, with colorful markets and a culture deeply influenced by its warm weather.

name: Darunta Lake
desc_short: Scenic reservoir with a mountain backdrop.
desc_long: Darunta Lake is a popular recreation spot near Jalalabad. Framed by rugged hills, the lake offers a peaceful waterfront setting for boat rides and riverside dining, serving as a vital irrigation source for the eastern plains.

name: Hadda Archaeological Site
desc_short: Lost monasteries of the Buddhist era.
desc_long: Hadda was once a thriving center of Greco-Buddhist art and pilgrimage. Although much has been lost to time, the site remains a critical location for understanding the spread of Buddhism and the unique 'Gandharan' art style that flourished here.

name: Gandamak Last Stand Site
desc_short: A hilltop steeped in 'Great Game' history.
desc_long: Gandamak is famously known as the site of the final stand of the British 44th Regiment during the 1842 retreat from Kabul. Today, the quiet hilltop provides a place to reflect on the immense scale of history that has passed through these eastern passes.

name: Nimroz Citadel
desc_short: A monumental mud-brick desert ruin.
desc_long: Located in the Chakhansur district, this massive citadel was once the seat of power in the Sistan basin. Built from thousands of sun-dried bricks, its ruins rise dramatically from the flat desert plains, testifying to a 2,000-year history of power and survival.

name: Qala Muhammad
desc_short: Traditional dwellings of the southwest.
desc_long: This village site near Zaranj offers a rare look at the unique desert architecture of southwestern Afghanistan. The use of wind-towers and thick mud walls showcases how local communities have adapted to the extreme heat of the Sistan desert for centuries.

name: Kamal Khan Dam
desc_short: A modern engineering marvel on the Helmand.
desc_long: This major dam project on the Helmand River is a pillar of southwestern development. It has transformed the arid landscape into a burgeoning agricultural zone, creating a new lakeside environment that is becoming a local landmark for progress.

name: Parun Valley
desc_short: The capital of 'The Land of Light'.
desc_long: Parun is the heart of Nuristan, a region of incredible isolation and unique wooden architecture. The valley is famous for its intricate timber-framed homes and a culture that remained distinct for centuries, set within a landscape of dense forests and rushing rivers.

name: Bashgal Valley
desc_short: Traditional wooden homes and mountain paths.
desc_long: Bashgal is known for its classic Nuristani villages, where multi-story wooden houses are stacked on steep hillsides. The valley offers an unparalleled cultural experience, highlighting a region where craftsmanship and mountain life are deeply intertwined.

name: Barg-e Matal & Lakes
desc_short: Remote alpine meadows and cedar forests.
desc_long: Barg-e Matal is one of the most remote settlements in the country. It is a gateway to high-altitude lakes and pristine cedar forests, offering an untouched wilderness experience for the most adventurous nature lovers.

name: Gardez & Zurmat Highlands
desc_short: Rugged mountains and pine-forested ridges.
desc_long: The Paktia highlands are defined by their rugged beauty and a landscape of scattered pine forests. The region is home to traditional Pashtun tribal communities and offers a glimpse into a culture deeply connected to the mountain terrain of the southeast.

name: Paktika Highlands
desc_short: Vast frontier landscapes and tribal culture.
desc_long: Paktika is a land of wide, high-altitude plains and dispersed tribal villages. The province offers a sense of the vast, open frontier of southeastern Afghanistan, where life is governed by ancient tribal codes and the rhythm of the seasons.

name: Ahmad Shah Massoud Mausoleum
desc_short: The domed monument of the Panjshir.
desc_long: Perched on a hill overlooking the valley, this domed mausoleum commemorates the 'Lion of Panjshir'. It has become a site of national significance, offering visitors a place of reflection and the most iconic panoramic view of the Panjshir River and its lush banks.

name: Paryan Valley
desc_short: Pristine alpine trekking and river headwaters.
desc_long: Located at the far end of the Panjshir, Paryan is a high-altitude paradise for hikers. With crystal-clear mountain streams, wildflower meadows, and peaks that remain snow-capped year-round, it offers some of the best alpine scenery in central Afghanistan.

name: Salang Pass
desc_short: The high-altitude gateway to the north.
desc_long: Traversing the Hindu Kush at over 3,400 meters, the Salang Pass is one of the world's most dramatic mountain crossings. The route is defined by steep switchbacks, massive snow galleries, and the engineering marvel of the Salang Tunnel, offering rugged scenery and crisp alpine air.

name: Jabal Saraj Palace
desc_short: Royal-era fortress at the foot of the pass.
desc_long: Built by King Habibullah Khan in the early 20th century, this historic palace served as a winter retreat and strategic lookout. Its architecture blends traditional Afghan fortress styles with modern influences, standing as a landmark in the fertile plains of Parwan.

name: Takht-e Rostam
desc_short: Unique rock-cut Buddhist stupa.
desc_long: Unlike any other stupa in the world, Takht-e Rostam is carved entirely into the bedrock rather than built on top of it. This monolithic 4th-century structure, shaped like a massive stone bowl, is surrounded by a deep trench and adjacent rock-cut cave monasteries, creating a surreal and spiritual atmosphere.

name: Buddhist Cave Monasteries
desc_short: Rock-cut cells of ancient Silk Road monks.
desc_long: Adjacent to the Takht-e Rostam stupa, these carved caves served as the living and meditation quarters for Buddhist monks. Exploring the hollowed chambers gives visitors a tangible sense of the monastic life that once flourished along this branch of the Silk Road.

name: Sar-e-Pol Meadows
desc_short: Vast rolling hills and pastoral plains.
desc_long: Sar-e-Pol is a region of immense, open meadows that serve as a corridor for nomadic movement in the north. The landscape is characterized by its scale and simplicity, offering wide horizons and a peaceful view of Afghanistan's northern pastoral life.

name: Ai-Khanoum
desc_short: The 'Moon City' of the Greeks.
desc_long: Situated at the confluence of the Oxus and Kokcha rivers, this site was a major Hellenistic city. It represents the easternmost reach of Greek culture, where archaeological remains have revealed a gymnasium, a theater, and temples that blend Mediterranean and Oriental traditions.

name: Tarin Kot Surroundings
desc_short: Rugged valleys and mud-brick villages.
desc_long: Uruzgan is a province of stark, mountainous beauty. The area surrounding Tarin Kot is home to traditional village communities where life is deeply rooted in the land. It offers a raw and authentic look at the rural south, away from modern urban centers.

name: Wardak Highlands
desc_short: High valleys and historic stone settlements.
desc_long: Wardak is defined by its high-altitude valleys and historic mud and stone architecture. Located along a major national corridor, it offers beautiful mountain scenery and a glimpse into the agricultural life of the central highlands.

name: Shahjoy & Qalat Valleys
desc_short: Southern mountain ridges and tribal lands.
desc_long: Zabul is a land of rugged ridges and dry valleys. The landscape is dotted with ancient fortresses and traditional settlements, offering a glimpse into the resilient tribal cultures that have inhabited these southern passes for generations.
